topics: Labor policy and prevailing wage trade-offs
Local government resistance to state housing mandates
Prop 13 and fiscalization of land use

claims.claim: The cost per square foot of constructing an apartment in California is over twice that of constructing it in Texas, according to a RAND study.
evidence: RAND study cited by Ezra Klein
California multifamily takes 49 months vs 27 months in Texas and 37 months in Colorado

claims.claim: Speed of permitting and approval—not labor or materials alone—is the primary driver of higher construction costs in California.
evidence: RAND study shows California takes 49 months vs Colorado's 27 months
Faster timelines could cut 10-20% off market-rate housing price
Katie Porter argues others haven't read the RAND study closely enough

claims.claim: Paying prevailing wage in residential construction increases the cost per unit by approximately $94,000.
evidence: Analysis from the Turner Center cited by Ezra Klein

claims.claim: Affordable housing costs roughly four times as much per square foot to construct in California as market-rate housing costs in Texas.
evidence: RAND study data cited by Ezra Klein: market-rate California is 2x Texas; affordable housing in California is 4x Texas market-rate

claims.claim: San Jose approved 22,000 homes that largely were not built because the economics didn't work, but cutting fees by over two-thirds led to 2,000 homes getting under construction in one year.
evidence: Mayor Matt Mahan's account of San Jose fee reductions
2,000 additional homes securing financing after fee cuts

claims.claim: Local impact fees add 10-20% to the cost of housing and are a primary lever that cities and counties control.
evidence: Matt Mahan cites over 10 pages of fees in San Jose
Tom Steyer states cities charge fees up to 20% of construction cost
Antonio Villaraigosa says impact fees are 'killing us'

claims.claim: Modular/offsite construction companies have repeatedly failed because they lacked sufficient orders, not because the technology doesn't work.
evidence: Katerra raised $2 billion in private capital and went bankrupt
Veve and Intecra also failed
Factory OS was rebranded and recapitalized
Tom Steyer argues state orders and building code changes can fix the revenue problem

claims.claim: There are 40,000 permitted and zoned housing units in San Francisco that are not being built because construction costs make them financially unviable.
evidence: Cited by both Tom Steyer and Javier Becerra
Becerra links the $10 billion Wicks bond to unlocking these shovel-ready projects

quote_candidates.text: A RAND study found that the cost per square foot of constructing an apartment in California is over twice that of constructing it in Texas.
quote_candidates.speaker: Ezra Klein

quote_candidates.text: There are 40,000 units in San Francisco, California that are permitted, that are zoned, that are not being built because they can't afford to build them to a price that people can afford to buy them.
quote_candidates.speaker: Tom Steyer

quote_candidates.text: We should not believe that we have to build homes by making it so it's impossible for the carpenter who builds a home to never be able to afford to buy it.
quote_candidates.speaker: Javier Becerra

quote_candidates.text: The study is very, very clear that the speed is the driver.
quote_candidates.speaker: Katie Porter

quote_candidates.text: I am not scared of anybody because I've got three teenagers that I do not want living on my couch.
quote_candidates.speaker: Katie Porter

quote_candidates.text: We cut the fees by over two-thirds and 2,000 homes got under construction last year.
quote_candidates.speaker: Matt Mahan

guest_candidates.name: Tom Steyer
guest_candidates.title: Democratic Candidate for Governor of California

guest_candidates.name: Javier Becerra
guest_candidates.title: Democratic Candidate for Governor of California; former California Attorney General

guest_candidates.name: Katie Porter
guest_candidates.title: Democratic Candidate for Governor of California; U.S. Representative

guest_candidates.name: Matt Mahan
guest_candidates.title: Democratic Candidate for Governor of California; Mayor of San Jose

guest_candidates.name: Antonio Villaraigosa
guest_candidates.title: Democratic Candidate for Governor of California; former Mayor of Los Angeles

topics: California housing policy and CEQA reform
Homelessness prevention and interim housing strategies
State-city relations and fiscal incentives for housing
California Forever and master-planned community development
Mental health, coercion, and Housing First accountability

claims.claim: Litigation as a housing enforcement tool is not particularly effective at actually getting housing built
evidence: Huntington Beach is not building more housing despite legal pressure
A San Diego County project with 2,000 units sued under AG Becerra still hasn't been built

claims.claim: Measure ULA caused an 84% drop in construction in Los Angeles according to a RAND/UCLA study
evidence: ULA is a transfer tax on properties over $5 million including multifamily and commercial, not just mansions
RAND and UCLA study cited showing 84% construction drop

claims.claim: California's $24 billion spent on homelessness from 2018 to 2023 failed because outcomes were not tracked and accountability was absent
evidence: $24 billion spent mostly within Housing First framework
Unsheltered population continued to grow
Becerra: 'We didn't focus on outcomes'

claims.claim: Only one in seven people who becomes homeless has a pre-existing mental health problem, but virtually everyone who stays on the street long-term develops one
evidence: Being homeless is described as 'incredibly stressful, vulnerable and dangerous'
Steyer uses this to argue for rapid removal from streets as a mental health intervention

claims.claim: Direct cash assistance is the most effective and cost-efficient way to prevent eviction and foreclosure
evidence: Median cost of cash-based eviction/foreclosure prevention is $6,000 per family
Compare to $750,000–$1 million per unit for permanent assisted housing
Audacious Foundation funded pilots across California

claims.claim: Emergency interim housing—with private rooms, keys, pet allowances, and wraparound services—gets approximately 70% of unsheltered people off the street, far more than traditional shelters
evidence: Traditional shelters seen as dangerous, no privacy, no pets allowed
Steyer claims emergency interim housing acceptance rate around 70%
Permanent assisted housing costs $750,000–$1 million per key

claims.claim: San Jose's prevention model, partnered with Destination Home, kept over 92% of assisted households housed without ongoing public subsidy
evidence: Notre Dame University longitudinal study over 3–4 years
One-time rental assistance paired with case management
San Jose reduced inflow by 50% from streets
speakers: Mayor Mayhem (Mayor Matt Mahan of San Jose)

claims.claim: CEQA is broken because, unlike NEPA, it does not require environmental standing to sue, allowing anyone from anywhere to sue California projects
evidence: 'You can sue from Richmond, Virginia for a project in Richmond, California'
Stone Ranch project had 33 CEQA objections; after resolving 29, lost on one and had to restart all 33

quote_candidates.text: If you want to be popular, get a dog.
quote_candidates.speaker: Mr. Villaraigosa (Antonio Villaraigosa)

quote_candidates.text: Only one in seven people who becomes homeless has a mental health problem. But virtually everyone who stays on the street for a long time develops one.
quote_candidates.speaker: Mr. Steyer (Tom Steyer)

quote_candidates.text: You can sue from Richmond, Virginia for a project in Richmond, California. Yes, you can. It's broken.
quote_candidates.speaker: Mr. Villaraigosa (Antonio Villaraigosa)

quote_candidates.text: The opposite perception that we can't trust people with cash, we can't give low income people cash is racist. It's sexist and it's wrong. And it costs us a fortune.
quote_candidates.speaker: Ms. Porter (Katie Porter)

quote_candidates.text: We didn't focus on outcomes. There was the accountability wasn't there. Twenty four billion dollars was there, but the outcomes didn't result.
quote_candidates.speaker: Mr. Becerra (Xavier Becerra)

quote_candidates.text: No one gets well on the street.
quote_candidates.speaker: Mr. Steyer (Tom Steyer)

guest_candidates.name: Antonio Villaraigosa
guest_candidates.title: Former Mayor of Los Angeles, California Governor Candidate

guest_candidates.name: Xavier Becerra
guest_candidates.title: Former California Attorney General and U.S. Secretary of HHS, Governor Candidate

guest_candidates.name: Tom Steyer
guest_candidates.title: Businessman and Activist, California Governor Candidate

guest_candidates.name: Katie Porter
guest_candidates.title: Former U.S. Representative, California Governor Candidate

guest_candidates.name: Matt Mahan
guest_candidates.title: Mayor of San Jose, California Governor Candidate

topics: Homelessness policy and interim housing strategies
Affordable housing costs and construction inefficiency
Rent caps and the Tenant Protection Act
Mental health services and progressive governance critique
Book recommendations from California gubernatorial candidates

claims.claim: Establishing no-encampment zones around interim housing sites was necessary to gain community buy-in and show visible progress on homelessness.
evidence: Allowing people to camp a block away from interim sites 'visually undermines that trust and belief that we're making progress'
San Jose implemented this approach with support from colleagues described as having 'courage'

claims.claim: Moving people indoors into interim housing with case management caused 911 and 311 calls to plummet, improving overall quality of life.
evidence: Calls for service for Crime 911 and Blight 311 'plummeted' after people were stabilized indoors
Unmanaged tent encampments generate noise, fires, drug use, and associated service calls

claims.claim: Publicly subsidized new affordable housing construction is inefficient and unscalable compared to buying older housing stock or incentivizing the private market.
evidence: Local public construction costs are 30% higher than the private market
New public units cost ~$1 million each vs. older stock at $300,000 or private market builds at $600,000
Mayor Mahan says he would have encouraged buying older stock rather than building new

claims.claim: California spent $24 billion at the state level on homelessness, yet homelessness increased, with only two programs—rental assistance and Homekey—shown to work by the LAO.
evidence: $24 billion spent at the state level
Legislative Analyst Office (LAO) analysis found only rental assistance and Homekey were effective
Average permanent supportive housing unit in LA costs $850,000; in Santa Monica, $1.2 million

claims.claim: Rent caps are acceptable as a temporary measure but supply expansion is the only long-term solution to high rents.
evidence: The Tenant Protection Act was passed during COVID when 'people were starving'
Villaraigosa cites studies showing supply is needed to bring down rents long-term
Calls for market rate, workforce, affordable, and homeless housing simultaneously

claims.claim: Democrats have failed to build mental health facilities despite blaming Reagan for decades, and accountability for using care courts is lacking at the county level.
evidence: Democrats have been in office for 28 years without building mental facilities
Care courts exist but there is 'no accountability at the county level' to use them
Villaraigosa notes parents and spouses find it 'almost impossible' to institutionalize a loved one

claims.claim: Many people on the street developed drug addiction and mental illness after becoming homeless, not before, due to the trauma of homelessness.
evidence: Villaraigosa states many were not on drugs or mentally ill 'when they first got homeless'
Threats of rape, beatings, lack of food on the street lead people to drugs and mental illness

quote_candidates.text: what is compassionate, humane or progressive about people living in their poop? About people getting beat up at night and stabbed? There is nothing progressive about that, nothing.
quote_candidates.speaker: Antonio Villaraigosa

quote_candidates.text: if I could go back, I would have encouraged us to buy the older housing stock that's $300,000 a unit, rather than build new at a million a unit when the private marketplace could have built if we had just incentivized them at 600,000 a unit.
quote_candidates.speaker: Mayor Mahan

quote_candidates.text: We spent $24 billion at the state level, and the fact is, homelessness went up.
quote_candidates.speaker: Antonio Villaraigosa

quote_candidates.text: The average unit in LA for permanent supportive housing is $850,000. Your kids can't afford that. We can build tiny homes, we're doing it in San Jose, we're doing it in LA for $100,000.
quote_candidates.speaker: Antonio Villaraigosa

quote_candidates.text: Democrats have been in office for 28 years. Why don't we build mental facilities?
quote_candidates.speaker: Antonio Villaraigosa

quote_candidates.text: a lot of these people that are on drugs and mentally ill now, they weren't when they first got homeless, but after being homeless and threatened to be raped and beaten up and no food and everything else, yes, they start getting on drugs and they develop mental illnesses and a drug problem.
quote_candidates.speaker: Antonio Villaraigosa
